concept: Burden / Weight or Demand Borne by a Living Being
description: A weight, demand, or difficult responsibility borne by a living being. The word centers the bearer: the same object may be a technical 'takori' (load) for a structure, a 'ritako' (workload) across time, and a 'tupeka' for the horse or person under it. A second sentence can tell whether someone chose it and whether its distribution causes harm.
sound_symbolism: Dental 't' touches firmly before deep 'u' settles the first syllable low in the mouth. Stressed 'pe' closes the lips and releases them into a narrower vowel, then final 'ka' adds a second closure farther back before opening wide. The word asks the mouth to lift twice: once at the lips, once at the tongue, with the weight audible between them.
grammatical_notes: A countable or mass noun: 'kalora ta whalo tupeka kolua.' (The horse carries a great burden.) A burden may be physical or figurative. Compare 'tumoa' (heavy), which describes weight; 'takori' (load), which measures demand on a technical system; and 'ritako' (workload), which gathers work over an interval.

pillars.solarpunk-values: A plan can distribute materials efficiently and still place its hardest burden on one body or community. 'Tupeka' keeps that bearer inside the account.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Calling something a burden reports what must be borne without deciding that the demand was accepted or deserved.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: People and working animals carried the weight that roads, harnesses, poles, and wheels redistributed. The old techniques changed how a burden rested; they did not make the bearer disappear.

tags.activity: a difficult demand borne through action or responsibility
tags.community: weight or demand located on an affected bearer
tags.physical: a weight supported by a living body
